mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses how nations achieve economic development and escape poverty (A.4), the Solow growth model as a theory of economic growth (A.2), GDP and GNP metrics measuring growth rates versus population growth (A.4.a), government policy and institutional quality as factors influencing growth (A.1), social costs and benefits including sustainability and environmental concerns (A.3), and various theories of development and retardation (A.4.b). Role of the IMF and international organizations in economic planning for developing countries (B.4).

Resource allocation, human capital formation, and economies of scale as factors influencing economic growth (A.1), macroeconomic development measures (A), contrasting conditions between developed and developing nations illustrating economic growth disparities (A.4), and research and development contributing to changes in economic efficiency (A.5).

Comparative analysis of why some countries achieved prosperity while others remained poor, directly addressing factors influencing economic growth (A.1), economic growth in developing countries (A.4), and various theories of national economic development and retardation (A.4.b).

Innovation as a process driving economic development through technology and creative destruction as a factor influencing economic growth (A.1), and disruptive technologies affecting changes in output per unit of input as measures of economic productivity (A.5).

Technological innovations in steam power, textiles, and iron production as factors influencing economic growth (A.1), Britain's transformation as a model of economic growth theory (A.2), and differential impacts on social classes illustrating social costs and benefits of growth (A.3).

Capital accumulation, market forces, and industrialization as factors influencing economic growth (A.1), comparative capitalisms illustrating different growth models (A.2), and the relationship between market systems and economic expansion (A).

Communist centralized economic planning as the primary example of economic planning in Communist countries (B.2), covering the Soviet Union, China, Cuba, and other socialist states' approaches to planned growth.

Swedish social democracy as economic planning in a developed non-Communist country (B.3), and Cuban communism as economic planning in a Communist country (B.2), illustrating alternative approaches to economic growth and planning.
Keynesian economic theory as a demand-determined model of growth (A.2), and Keynesian fiscal policy as a framework for economic planning and stability in developed non-Communist countries (B.3).

Free markets, deregulation, and privatization as governmental fiscal policies influencing economic growth (A.1), and neoliberal policy implementation as a form of economic planning in developed non-Communist countries (B.3).

Cost-benefit analysis of environmental protection and carbon taxes directly addressing social costs and benefits of economic growth (A.3), examining the tradeoffs between economic development and environmental harm.
Material and energy flows in consumption and production, tipping points, and strategies for sustainable development directly addressing the social costs and benefits of economic growth (A.3).

The law of diminishing returns and population theory as a foundational growth model (A.2), the relationship between population growth and economic output (A.4.a), and Malthus's influence on theories of economic development and retardation (A.4).

The Wealth of Nations establishing foundational concepts about markets, trade, and competition as factors influencing economic growth (A.1), and as one of the earliest growth theories emphasizing supply-side factors (A.2).

Historical materialism and critique of political economy as a theory of economic growth and class dynamics (A.2), and Marxism as the theoretical foundation for economic planning in Communist countries (B.2).

Soviet centrally planned economy as a primary example of economic planning in Communist countries (B.2), and the post-Soviet transition examining economic development through state-controlled versus market-driven approaches (A.4).

BRICS nations as emerging economies challenging Western-dominated institutions, relevant to economic growth in developing countries (A.4), and the group's role in reshaping international economic planning and development lending (B.4).

Global trade, migration, and economic deregulation as factors influencing economic growth (A.1), and transnational flows affecting economic growth in developing countries through integration or exclusion (A.4).

Disproportionate environmental burdens on marginalized communities and arguments for changes to economic structures directly addressing social costs of economic growth and their unequal distribution (A.3).

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: Covers Roosevelt's New Deal interventionist programs and Keynesian economics — directly relevant to the nature of economic planning (unresolved leaf B.1) and planning in developed non-Communist countries.
Covers Stalinist industrialization and central planning — directly relevant to economic planning in Communist countries.
Covers China's transformation from central planning to market economics — relevant to economic growth in developing countries and planning in Communist countries.
